section.title: Concern for the Corinthians
section.sectionRationale: The fool's speech ends and the pastor's heart is laid bare. Paul insists he is in no way inferior to the super-apostles, for the marks of a true apostle were done among the Corinthians, yet he calls himself nothing. He will not be a burden; he seeks not their possessions but them, spending and being spent for their souls like a parent for children. The section closes with his fear that when he comes he will find quarreling, jealousy, and unrepented sin.
section.thesis: A true apostle is marked not by self-promotion but by self-giving love that seeks the people rather than their possessions, and by a holy jealousy for their repentance and growth in Christ.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the marks of a true apostle done among them; the parent who spends and is spent for the children's souls; and the pastor's fear of finding sin and disorder when he comes.

units.label: The marks of a true apostle
units.note: in no way inferior, yet nothing

verses.text: I have become a fool, but you drove me to it. In fact, you should have commended me, since I am in no way inferior to those “super-apostles,” even though I am nothing.
The marks of a true apostle, {{semeion|signs}}, wonders, and miracles, were performed among you with great perseverance.
In what way were you inferior to the other churches, except that I was not a burden to you? Forgive me this wrong!

units.label: I will spend and be spent

verses.text: See, I am ready to come to you a third time, and I will not be a burden, because I am not seeking your possessions, but you. For children should not have to save up for their parents, but parents for their children.
And for the sake of your souls, I will most gladly spend my money and myself. If I {{agapao|love}} you more, will you love me less?
Be that as it may, I was not a burden to you; but crafty as I am, I caught you by trickery.
Did I exploit you by anyone I sent you?
I urged Titus to visit you, and I sent our brother with him. Did Titus exploit you in any way? Did we not walk in the same Spirit and follow in the same footsteps?

units.label: Fear of finding sin
units.note: to build you up, not tear you down

verses.text: Have you been thinking all along that we were making a defense to you? We speak before God in Christ, and all of this, beloved, is to {{oikodome|build you up}}.

For I am afraid that when I come, I may not find you as I wish, and you may not find me as you wish. I fear that there may be quarreling, jealousy, rage, rivalry, slander, gossip, arrogance, and disorder.

I am afraid that when I come again, my God will humble me before you, and I will be grieved over many who have sinned earlier and have not repented of their acts of impurity, sexual immorality, and debauchery.

semeion.gloss: A sign, a token that points beyond itself to a deeper reality. Paul lists signs, wonders, and miracles as the marks of a true apostle worked among the Corinthians. A sign is never an end in itself; it authenticates the messenger and points to the God who sends him. Paul notes these were done with great perseverance, so that his very endurance was itself part of the credential.

agapao.gloss: To love with self-giving, sacrificial love, the love that seeks the good of the other regardless of return. Paul loves the Corinthians this way, gladly spending his money and himself for their souls even if they love him less. This is the love of a parent for a child, and ultimately the love of God in Christ, who gave Himself for those who could give nothing back.

oikodome.gloss: Building up, edification, the strengthening and growth of a person or community as a house is raised. Paul insists that everything he writes, even his sharp words, is aimed not at self-defense but at building up the Corinthians. The image runs through the letter: the authority Christ gave the apostle is for construction, not demolition, for the maturing of the church in the faith.

basic.context: Paul finishes the reluctant boasting and speaks plainly as their spiritual father. He says he is in no way behind the so-called super-apostles, for the true marks of an apostle, signs and wonders and patient endurance, were seen among them, yet he calls himself nothing. He refuses to be a financial burden, insisting he wants them and not their money, and he will gladly spend himself for their souls. Then he opens his heart about his fear: that when he comes he will find quarreling, jealousy, and people who have not repented of serious sin.
basic.hermeneutics: This section shifts from irony to earnest love. Read the boasting of chapters 11 and 12 as forced by the false teachers, and this passage as the reason behind it: Paul cares for these people. His refusal of support, his parent language, and his fear of finding sin all reveal what a true minister looks like. Verse 19 gives the key: all of this is to build them up, not to defend himself.
basic.originalLanguages: The marks of an apostle include semeion, signs, tokens that point beyond themselves to God. Paul's care is agapao, self-giving love that spends itself for the beloved. And the whole aim, even of his sharp words, is oikodome, building up, the raising of the church like a house rather than its tearing down.
basic.typology: Paul's model of a parent who spends and is spent for the children reflects the fatherhood of God and supremely the Son who gave Himself. As the prophets grieved over Israel's unrepentant sin, so Paul grieves over Corinth, standing in the line of shepherds who long for their people to return. His refusal to exploit contrasts with the hirelings and false shepherds of Scripture.
basic.christ: The self-spending love Paul shows is the love of Christ flowing through His servant, the One who did not seek what was ours but us, and who gave Himself for our souls. Paul speaks before God in Christ and works for the building up of Christ's body. His holy grief over sin mirrors the Lord who wept over an unrepentant city and calls His people to repentance and holiness.
basic.god: It is God whom Paul serves and before whom he speaks, and God who may humble him by the state he finds at Corinth. This is a God who cares about the moral life of His church, grieved by impurity and disorder, and who works through faithful ministers to build His people up. He is the God of a love that gives rather than grasps.
basic.crossReferences: John 10 contrasts the good shepherd who lays down his life with the hireling. 1 Thessalonians 2 shows Paul as a nursing mother and a father with his children. Romans 15 speaks of building up the neighbor rather than pleasing oneself.
basic.oneStory: Throughout Scripture God gives shepherds to His people and grieves when they exploit the flock, promising at last a Shepherd who gives Himself. Paul embodies that self-giving love, seeking the people and not their goods, spending himself for their souls. The story runs toward Christ, whose love builds up His church and calls her from sin into holiness.

context.literary: Verse 11 closes the fool's speech with a sigh, I have become a fool, but you drove me to it, and pivots from irony to tenderness. The parent imagery in verse 14 and the outpouring of verse 15 are among the warmest words in the letter, while the heavy vice lists of verses 20 and 21 return to the sober reality awaiting his visit. The whole unit is framed by the tension between his love and their unfinished repentance.
context.historical: Wandering teachers in the ancient world commonly took payment and status from their hearers, and the false apostles at Corinth likely did so. Paul's refusal of support, which some twisted as proof he was not a real apostle or as a cover for crafty dealing, was in fact a costly act of love. The mention of Titus and the brother points back to the collection delegation and the trust it required.
context.canonical: This passage joins the New Testament's portrait of genuine gospel ministry as self-giving rather than self-serving, alongside 1 Thessalonians 2 and John 10. The vice lists echo Paul's other catalogs of the works of the flesh, and the call to repentance keeps the church's holiness tied to the gospel of grace rather than severing the two.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show the Corinthians that Paul's boasting was forced and his heart is love, that he is a true apostle who seeks them and not theirs, and to warn them plainly to repent before he comes, so that his visit may be for building up rather than grief.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The specific details, the third visit, the refusal of support, the sending of Titus, belong to Paul's situation. The abiding principles are prescriptive: true ministry is self-giving love that seeks people not possessions, aims at building up, and calls the church to repentance and holiness.

debates.question: Why did Paul refuse financial support from the Corinthians?

views.label: To avoid any charge of exploiting them
views.case: Paul repeatedly denies exploiting them and stresses he seeks them, not theirs, guarding the gospel from any hint of greed like the false teachers.

views.label: As a father's provision for his children
views.case: He frames it with the parent image: parents save for children, so he gladly spends himself for their souls rather than taking from them.

debates.bearing: Both are true and complementary; Paul's refusal was a deliberate act of self-giving love that also protected the gospel's integrity, not a denial that ministers may be supported.

debates.question: How can Paul both affirm the Corinthians as believers and fear finding them in serious sin?

views.label: Genuine believers can fall into grave sin needing repentance
views.case: Paul calls them beloved and speaks of building them up, yet warns of unrepented immorality, showing that real Christians can lapse and must be called back.

views.label: Some among them may not be truly converted
views.case: The following chapter urges them to examine whether they are in the faith, so the fear may include those whose profession is not real.

debates.bearing: The passage holds both a warm confidence in the church and a sober call to repentance; love does not ignore sin, and the call to repent is itself an act of pastoral care for the beloved.

study.originalLanguages: The credentials Paul names are semeion, signs, along with wonders and miracles, done with patient endurance; a sign never terminates on itself but points to the God who authenticates His messenger. His motive is agapao, the self-giving love that gladly spends money and self for the beloved's soul, the love of a parent and ultimately of God in Christ. And the goal of every word, even the cutting warnings, is oikodome, building up, raising the church like a house rather than tearing it down, so that his coming authority serves construction and not ruin.

typology.type: The parent who spends and is spent for the children
typology.fulfillment: Reflects the fatherhood of God and the Son who sought not ours but us, giving Himself for our souls.

typology.type: The shepherd grieved over unrepentant sin
typology.fulfillment: Stands in the line of the prophets and points to Christ, who wept over the impenitent and calls His people to repentance and holiness.

study.christ: Christ is the source and pattern of the love Paul displays. The apostle who seeks the people and not their possessions, who gladly spends and is spent for their souls, is showing the very heart of the Lord who did not come to be served but to give His life. Paul speaks before God in Christ, labors for the building up of Christ's body, and grieves over sin as the One who called sinners to repent. His self-giving, even when loved less in return, is the shape of Calvary love flowing through a servant.
study.god: God is the One before whom Paul speaks and serves, and whose honor is at stake in the holiness of the church. He is a God who so loves His people that He gives them shepherds who spend themselves, and who is grieved by impurity, quarreling, and pride among His own. He may humble even His apostle through the state of the church, showing that God cares more for the reality of His people's walk than for the reputation of His servants.

crossReferences.note: Paul among the churches as a nursing mother and an exhorting father, sharing not only the gospel but his very self.
The good shepherd lays down his life, unlike the hireling who cares nothing for the sheep.
We are to build up the neighbor and not please ourselves, following Christ who did not please Himself.
The works of the flesh, including the very sins Paul fears at Corinth, echoing his vice list here.

study.oneStory: God has always given His people shepherds and grieved when they fed themselves instead of the flock, promising a Shepherd who would lay down His life. Paul embodies that promised love, seeking the people and not their goods, spending himself for their souls, longing for their holiness. The story moves toward Christ, whose self-giving love builds up His church and calls her out of sin into the wholeness God intends.

quiz.q: What does Paul point to as the marks of a true apostle?

options.text: Signs, wonders, and miracles done among them with great perseverance.
options.note: Verse 12 names these as the marks worked among the Corinthians.

options.text: His impressive speaking ability and personal charm.
options.note: Paul actually declined to compete on eloquence and outward impressiveness.

options.text: The large sums of money he collected from them.
options.note: Paul refused their support rather than gathering money for himself.

options.text: The letters of recommendation he carried.
options.note: The marks Paul cites are the works done among them and his endurance, not letters.

quiz.q: Why does Paul say he will not be a burden to the Corinthians?

options.text: Because he seeks them, not their possessions, spending himself like a parent for children.
options.note: Verses 14 and 15 give the parent image and his motive.

options.text: Because he had more than enough money of his own.
options.note: His refusal was about love and integrity, not personal wealth.

options.text: Because he thought they were too poor to help.
options.note: The reason is his self-giving love, not an assessment of their poverty.

options.text: Because an apostle is never allowed to receive support.
options.note: Paul elsewhere affirms support is legitimate; here he waives it out of love.

quiz.q: What does Paul say is the purpose of everything he writes?

options.text: To build them up, speaking before God in Christ, not to defend himself.
options.note: Verse 19 states the aim of edification.

options.text: To win the argument against the false apostles.
options.note: His concern is their growth, not merely defeating opponents.

options.text: To clear his own name at any cost.
options.note: He explicitly says it is not self-defense but their building up.

options.text: To make them feel guilty enough to obey.
options.note: The goal is edification in love, not manipulation through guilt.

quiz.q: What does Paul fear he will find when he comes to Corinth?

options.text: Quarreling, jealousy, and many who have not repented of impurity and immorality.
options.note: Verses 20 and 21 list the sins and the unrepentance he dreads.

options.text: A church that has become too wealthy and comfortable.
options.note: His fear is moral and relational sin, not prosperity.

options.text: That they will have forgotten his teaching entirely.
options.note: The specific fear is unrepented sin and disorder, not mere forgetfulness.

options.text: That the false apostles will physically attack him.
options.note: His grief is over the Corinthians' sin, not a threat of violence.

questions: Paul could claim real credentials yet called himself nothing. How do you hold together honest confidence in what God has done through you and genuine humility before Him?
Paul sought the Corinthians themselves, not their possessions. In your relationships and ministry, are you seeking people or what you can get from them?
He was willing to spend and be spent even if loved less in return. Where is God calling you to a love that keeps giving without guarantee of return?
Paul insists his hard words are meant to build up, not tear down. How do you receive correction, and how do you give it, so that it builds rather than destroys?
Paul grieved over unrepented sin among people he loved. Is there sin you have been leaving unrepented, and what would it mean to turn from it before God today?

translationNotes.note: The phrase those super-apostles is Paul's irony for the intruding false teachers; the BSB keeps the term in quotation marks, and the clause is rendered without dashes, even though I am nothing.
The list signs, wonders, and miracles is set off in the original with breaks; it is rendered here with commas to avoid dashes, so the marks of a true apostle read as a smooth series.
The BSB footnote notes a more literal reading, did we not walk in the same Spirit, not in the same footsteps; the sense is that Paul and Titus acted with the same integrity toward the Corinthians.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 2 Corinthians 12:11-21. Em dashes removed and replaced with commas or clean breaks, notably the appositive lists in 12:11 and 12:12. Curly quotes retained around super-apostles in 12:11. Footnote markers stripped. Sermon set to none; no sermon URL verified this session.
